acholuria: A jaundiced patient produces pale, straw-colored urine rather than the tea-brown often expected. The urine pigment is missing because the offending bile pigment can't be filtered by the kidney.
anuria: Kidney output collapses below a hundred milliliters in a day — one step past oliguria, and past what dehydration alone can explain. Points to acute injury or complete obstruction of the drainage tract.
balanitis: Redness and itching of the tip of the male organ, common in uncircumcised men and diabetics. The Greek stem borrows the word for acorn.
bordetella: A prolonged coughing illness in infants that historically killed hundreds of thousands of children before routine vaccination. The organism is named for the Belgian microbiologist who cultured it.
bronchiectasis: A childhood infection or a chronic mucus-clearance disorder leaves the airway tubes permanently baggy and pooled with sputum. High-resolution CT shows characteristic 'signet ring' cross-sections.
burns: Depth is graded from superficial to full-thickness; in adults, the rule of nines estimates total body surface involvement. Fluid resuscitation drives the first day of care.
cachexia: A single Greek-rooted noun for the gaunt, muscle-wasted appearance of a late-stage cancer patient. Inflammatory cytokines drive the catabolism even when calories still go in.
catatonia: A patient stops speaking, stops moving, and holds an arm in the position an examiner placed it for hours without complaint. First described on psychiatric wards; now recognized in autoimmune encephalitis too.
cheilosis: Painful cracks at the corners of the mouth, often from riboflavin or iron shortage. The word borrows the Greek for lip.
choledocholithiasis: Sudden right-upper-quadrant pain with dark urine and pale stools points to a blockage where a hollow storage organ shares plumbing with a nearby digestive gland.
cholecystectomy: The definitive treatment for recurrent right-upper-quadrant biliary colic — done through four small ports and a scope. The Greek roots read as 'cutting out the bile bladder.'
choroiditis: Floaters and blurred vision arise when the vascular carpet lying between the retina and the sclera inflames. Toxoplasmosis and sarcoid are classic drivers.
colitis: Endoscopy shows a friable, erythematous mucosa in a lower-abdominal viscus, and biopsies distinguish infectious from autoimmune from ischemic causes. The noun for the inflamed organ is the same in all three.
covid: A viral respiratory illness first identified in late 2019, notorious for a loss of smell and taste in its early wave, and for reshaping global public health.
crigler: An inherited enzyme deficiency prevents the liver from conjugating a yellow pigment, producing severe jaundice from birth. The eponym pairs two mid-20th-century American pediatricians.
dacryoadenitis: Painful swelling of a paired secretory structure just above the outer eye can result from viral, bacterial, or autoimmune inflammation.
dislocation: A shoulder pops out of its socket after an outstretched-arm fall — the joint no longer articulates. Reduction on the field, imaging afterwards to rule out fracture.
dissociation: A trauma survivor describes feeling detached from their own body, or losing chunks of time, or watching themselves from outside. The umbrella psychiatric term is a single noun.
dupuytrens: A slow, fibrotic contracture of a palmar structure gradually curls the ring and little digits inward. Disproportionately affects men of Northern European descent; named for a Napoleonic-era French surgeon.
dysgeusia: Chemotherapy, zinc deficiency, and certain medications distort or blunt one of the classical five senses in a way patients often describe as metallic.
dysmenorrhea: Cyclical crampy lower-pelvic pain that peaks in the first days of a monthly physiologic event. Prostaglandin-driven and responsive to NSAIDs.
dysuria: Burning during urination that sends a young woman to clinic. A dipstick showing nitrites and leukocyte esterase points straight to a lower urinary tract infection.
ecchymosis: A larger, flat, non-blanching skin discoloration from extravasated red cells appears blue-black at first and evolves through green and yellow as heme pigments break down.
ecthyma: A deeper, ulcerative form of a superficial bacterial skin infection produces punched-out crusted lesions, most often in children and immunocompromised adults.
epiglottitis: Sudden high fever, drooling, and a muffled voice in a child who prefers to sit forward — a true airway emergency, once tied to a bacterium now curbed by routine immunization.
erysipelas: A brightly demarcated, fiery-red patch on the face or shin, raised at its edge like a coastline drawn on skin. Group A strep is the usual driver; the classical name predates germ theory.
erythema: A general term for redness of the skin from dilated capillaries — the noun stem hides inside many named rashes tied to drugs, infections, and autoimmune disease.
euvolemia: The Goldilocks state of intravascular volume — not dry, not overloaded. A clinical judgment call that decides how much saline to run.
fibrosis: The scarring endpoint of many chronic injuries — the lungs of a coal miner, the liver of a heavy drinker — where working tissue is replaced by unyielding collagen.
fungemia: A yeast is grown from a blood culture drawn from an ICU line. The septic picture that follows can be as deadly as its bacterial cousin; Candida is by far the commonest culprit.
gliadin: A protein fraction of wheat gluten that triggers small-bowel villus flattening in a genetically susceptible patient.
hordeolum: A tender red bump at the base of an eyelash, from a blocked sebaceous or sweat gland of the lid, resolves with a warm compress. The Latin name is a barley grain; the everyday English word for it is much shorter.
hyperaldosteronism: Resistant hypertension with persistently low potassium in an otherwise healthy 40-year-old. The adrenal cortex is oversecreting a mineralocorticoid — sometimes from a solitary Conn adenoma.
kwashiorkor: A weaned toddler in a famine develops a swollen belly, thinning reddish hair, and peeling flaking skin. The word comes from a Ghanaian language — 'the sickness the older child gets when the next is born.'
laryngomalacia: Inspiratory stridor in a newborn that softens and disappears by about eighteen months as the cartilage stiffens. The most common cause of noisy breathing in infants, and reassuringly self-limited.
lassa: A West African arenavirus carried by the multimammate rat, producing high fever, sore throat, and hemorrhagic shock. Named for a Nigerian town where it was first identified in 1969.
lymphedema: One arm swells for months after axillary node dissection for breast cancer, and never fully resolves. Obstructed drainage returns clear fluid to the tissues where it collects and firms up.
melanoma: A pigmented skin lesion that changes in size, color, or border can hide the deadliest of the common skin cancers. Strongly driven by ultraviolet exposure.
molluscum: A poxvirus produces clusters of small, dome-shaped, umbilicated bumps on the skin that spread among children and clear on their own over months to years.
mucocele: A soft, bluish, painless swelling on the inside of a lower lip after a minor bite — a benign collection of trapped secretions from a minor gland.
muscular: An adjective, often paired with 'dystrophy' — a family of inherited disorders that progressively wastes skeletal fibers.
nosocomial: A pneumonia contracted on the fifth day of a hospital admission is named for its point of origin, not its organism.
oligodendroglioma: A middle-aged adult presents with a first seizure; imaging shows a slow-growing frontal-lobe mass flecked with calcifications. Its cell of origin normally wraps neurons in insulating myelin.
orthopnea: A patient sleeps propped on three pillows because breath comes easier upright. Points to left-sided heart failure — recumbent posture returns blood to a ventricle that can't handle it.
parkinsonism: A slowing of movement with a resting tremor, cogwheel rigidity, and a shuffling gait defines a syndrome named for a nineteenth-century London physician who described its 'shaking palsy.'
peliosis: Small blood-filled cavities scattered through the liver of a bodybuilder abusing anabolic steroids. In immunocompromised patients, a Bartonella infection produces the same speckled pattern.
periodontitis: Chronic inflammation of the tissues that anchor the teeth destroys supporting bone. Modern evidence links this common oral disease to cardiovascular risk.
petechiae: Press a glass slide against the rash of a febrile child — if the fine red pinpricks don't fade under pressure, the pediatrician moves fast. A pattern of skin bleeding seen in low-platelet states and in meningococcal sepsis.
phthisis: A nineteenth-century poet coughs blood, wastes to skeletal thinness, and dies in a sanatorium. The Greek-rooted name for the disease predates identification of the bacillus by a full century.
plague: A rodent-associated bacterial infection with buboes, fever, and sometimes blackened extremities famously killed a third of medieval Europe and still occurs in rural pockets of the western United States.
polyneuritis: A symmetric burning-and-tingling sensory loss of the feet climbing up the legs suggests widespread inflammation of many peripheral nerves, often driven by diabetes, alcohol, or toxins.
polyneuropathy: A stocking-and-glove pattern of numbness and burning that ascends over years. Diabetes leads the causes; alcohol, B12 deficiency, and platinum chemotherapy round out the differential.
pyoderma: A generic descriptor for a pus-producing skin infection also names a striking ulcerative autoimmune disorder that responds to steroids rather than antibiotics.
rickets: Bowed legs and beaded ribs in a child who has spent little time in the sun. Nineteenth-century English factory towns saw the deformity often enough that it took an English name.
sarcopenia: The gradual thinning of skeletal muscle that turns brisk elderly walkers into cautious ones. Not the outcome of a single disease — the wasting of time itself.
sialadenitis: A firm painful swelling under the jaw that worsens at mealtime, sometimes on smelling a lemon. A duct stone from a paired mouth-fed gland is the classic trigger.
strangulated: An adjective, applied most often to a trapped bowel loop or an incarcerated hernia, warns that the blood supply is cut off and tissue death is imminent without emergency intervention.
takayasu: A large-vessel arteritis of young women that inflames and narrows the aorta and its main branches, sometimes producing absent pulses in the arms. Named for a Japanese ophthalmologist.
takotsubo: Sudden apex-ballooning of the left ventricle after an intense emotional shock mimics a heart attack in older women. Named for a Japanese pot used to catch octopus.
thromboembolism: A clot forms in a deep leg vein, breaks free, and lodges in the lung — one compound word for the whole cascade. Immobility, oral contraceptives, and cancer are classic triggers.
tracheomalacia: Abnormal softness of the cartilaginous rings supporting the main airway causes a barking cough and stridor that worsens with crying in infants and with exertion in adults.
ureteric: An adjective for the paired muscular tubes that carry urine from kidney to bladder — often paired with words like colic, stone, or reflux.
vaginitis: Itching, discharge, and sometimes a fishy or yeasty smell — the lower female tract can inflame from bacterial overgrowth, candida, or trichomonads, and the noun for the inflamed organ is the same in all three.
varicose: Long-haul flight attendants and cashiers on their feet all day know the ropey, blue-green cords that surface on the calves. Failing venous valves let a column of blood settle downward against gravity.
volvulus: A loop of bowel twists on its mesentery and cuts off both flow and blood supply. In elderly patients the sigmoid colon is the classic site; on the abdominal film it draws the shape of a coffee bean.
vulvovaginitis: A prepubertal or a postmenopausal patient presents with external genital soreness and discharge — thin estrogen support of the mucosa lets normal flora shift and inflame the lower tract.
whipple: A pancreatic-head cancer is resected by removing duodenum, gallbladder, part of the stomach, and the head of the pancreas in a single specimen — an operation named for the surgeon who also described a bacterial malabsorption syndrome he studied at autopsy.
whooping: A characteristic inspiratory sound following paroxysms of dry cough gives its evocative name to a childhood bacterial infection prevented by a triple childhood vaccine.
